title: S.B. 213 to provide presumptive eligibility for workers’ compensation for RNs and other health care workers who are injured on the job, including exposure to Covid-19
prompt_preview:

Output JSON only matching the provided schema.
Return concise, standalone claim_texts that could be tested against scientific literature. Exclude purely procedural statem

raw_text:
```json
{
  "claims": [
    "California Nurses Association cosponsored S.B. 213 to provide presumptive eligibility for workers' compensation for RNs and other health care workers.",
    "Nurses have no presumption of eligibility for workers' compensation despite being at high risk of injury and illness.",
    "S.B. 213 will ensure that nurses and other health care workers are automatically eligible for workers' compensation if injured on the job or if they contract Covid-19.",
    "Presumptive eligibility for workers' compensation means certain injuries or infections are presumed to be job-related.",
    "Workers' compensation presumptions exist for first responder professions because they face exposure to numerous potential illnesses.",
    "As of Feb. 8, 2021, there were 89,507 confirmed Covid-19 cases among nurses and health care workers in California.",
    "There have been 32 fatalities among nurses in California due to Covid-19.",
    "Nurses have reported being told to attribute their Covid-19 infections to community exposure instead of their job.",
    "S.B. 213 aims to modernize outdated California laws regarding workers' compensation for nurses.",
    "The bill will create a rebuttable presumption for hospital employees providing direct patient care for various health issues, including infectious diseases and respiratory disorders."
  ]
}
```
